glossary.def: A bottom-up clustering method that starts with every point as its own cluster and repeatedly merges the two closest clusters until one remains, producing a full nested hierarchy rather than a single flat partition. The complete sequence of n-1 merges is recorded as a dendrogram.

The binary tree output of hierarchical clustering: leaves are the data points and each internal node is a merge drawn at a height equal to the linkage distance at which it occurred. Cutting it with a horizontal line at some height yields a flat clustering, with large vertical gaps marking the most stable places to cut.

The rule for measuring the distance between two clusters (not two points) in agglomerative clustering. Single linkage uses the nearest pair (chains along filaments), complete linkage the farthest pair (forces compact balls), and Ward linkage the pair whose merge increases within-cluster squared error least.

Density-Based Spatial Clustering of Applications with Noise: defines a cluster as a connected region of high point density of any shape, controlled by a radius eps and a minimum-neighbour count minPts. It needs no preset cluster count, finds arbitrarily shaped clusters, and uniquely assigns low-density points an explicit noise label.

DBSCAN's three point types. A core point has at least minPts neighbours within radius eps; a border point is within eps of a core point but not itself dense; a noise point is neither and is excluded from every cluster — the built-in outlier handling that distance-to-centre methods lack.

A probabilistic clustering model that treats the data as generated by k weighted Gaussian components, each with its own mean and (optionally full) covariance, so clusters can be tilted ellipses of any aspect ratio. It assigns each point a soft, fractional responsibility to every component rather than a hard label; k-means is its spherical, hard-assignment limit.

The iterative algorithm that fits a GMM by alternating an E-step (fix parameters, compute each point's responsibilities) and an M-step (fix responsibilities, re-estimate weights, means and covariances as weighted statistics). Each full iteration provably never decreases the data log-likelihood, converging to a local maximum that depends on initialization.

An internal cluster-validation measure: for each point, (b - a) / max(a, b), where a is the mean distance to its own cluster and b the mean distance to the nearest other cluster, giving a value in [-1, 1]. Averaged over all points it scores a whole clustering, but being distance-to-centre based it favours convex, k-means-shaped clusters and under-rates correct density-based partitions.

refs.cite: Ester, M., Kriegel, H.-P., Sander, J. & Xu, X. (1996). A Density-Based Algorithm for Discovering Clusters in Large Spatial Databases with Noise. Proc. 2nd Int. Conf. on Knowledge Discovery and Data Mining (KDD-96).
Dempster, A. P., Laird, N. M. & Rubin, D. B. (1977). Maximum Likelihood from Incomplete Data via the EM Algorithm. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society, Series B 39(1).
Rousseeuw, P. J. (1987). Silhouettes: A Graphical Aid to the Interpretation and Validation of Cluster Analysis. Journal of Computational and Applied Mathematics 20.
Ward, J. H. (1963). Hierarchical Grouping to Optimize an Objective Function. Journal of the American Statistical Association 58(301).
Schwarz, G. (1978). Estimating the Dimension of a Model. Annals of Statistics 6(2).

Campello, R. J. G. B., Moulavi, D., Zimek, A. & Sander, J. (2015). Hierarchical Density Estimates for Data Clustering, Visualization, and Outlier Detection (HDBSCAN). ACM Transactions on Knowledge Discovery from Data 10(1).
Hubert, L. & Arabie, P. (1985). Comparing Partitions. Journal of Classification 2(1).
